---
title: "Orthogonal Projection onto a Subspace"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # vectors / data
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # the highlighted vector / quantity (projection, line)
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # residual / orthogonality highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0)):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center")
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the **keystone** of the Geometry of Data
> track — the single operation the rest of the series leans on hardest. It assumes
> [GEOM-02 *Span and Subspaces*] (a subspace is the set of all linear combinations of
> some vectors — a line or plane through the origin), [GEOM-03 *Linear Independence,
> Basis, and Dimension*] (a basis is a minimal spanning set, and "independent columns"
> means no redundancy), and especially [GEOM-04 *The Dot Product*] and [GEOM-05
> *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*], which gave us length
> $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert=\sqrt{\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}}$, the angle relation
> $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}=\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta$, and
> the meaning of *perpendicular* ($\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}=0$). We use matrix notation
> for $A$, $A^\top$, and $(A^\top A)^{-1}$ lightly here; the full algebra of matrices as
> maps is built in [GEOM-08]–[GEOM-11], and we will name every matrix fact we borrow so the
> geometry stays self-contained. It sets up [GEOM-07 *Gram–Schmidt and QR*] (an
> orthonormal basis makes projection trivial), [GEOM-12 *The Four Subspaces*], and
> [GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21] (the SVD and pseudoinverse, the numerically stable projection). Most
> directly, it is the article that **Regression's least-squares geometry stands on** —
> [REGR-04 *Regression as Projection*] and [REGR-05 *Multiple Regression*] are this article
> wearing statistical clothes.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

You have a point $\mathbf{b}$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ and a subspace $S$ — a line, a plane, or
their higher-dimensional cousins — and $\mathbf{b}$ does not lie in $S$. Which point of
$S$ is *closest* to $\mathbf{b}$? This article defends one claim: the answer is found by
**dropping a perpendicular**, and that single geometric instruction determines everything.
We derive projection onto a single vector from scratch, prove the residual is orthogonal
to that vector, then generalize: projecting onto the span of the columns of a matrix $A$
forces the residual to be orthogonal to *every* column, which is exactly the matrix
equation $A^\top(\mathbf{b}-A\hat{\mathbf{c}})=\mathbf{0}$ — the **normal equations**
$A^\top A\,\hat{\mathbf{c}}=A^\top\mathbf{b}$. Solving them produces the **projection
matrix** $P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$, which we prove is symmetric and idempotent
($P^2=P$). We close the loop by proving that the perpendicular foot is genuinely the
*nearest* point — so "least distance" and "right angle" are the same condition. This is
why least squares is projection, why the residual is orthogonal to the fit, and why
$P^2=P$ means "projecting twice changes nothing."

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Given a point $\mathbf{b}$ and a subspace $S$, the closest point of $S$ to $\mathbf{b}$ is
the **orthogonal projection** $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ — the foot of the perpendicular dropped
from $\mathbf{b}$ onto $S$. Its defining property is not "it is closest" but the *reason*
it is closest: the leftover piece, the **residual** $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}$,
is **orthogonal to all of $S$**. When $S$ is the column space of a matrix $A$ with
independent columns, that orthogonality condition is the **normal equations** $A^\top
A\,\hat{\mathbf{c}}=A^\top\mathbf{b}$, and the projection is computed by the matrix
$$P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top,\qquad \hat{\mathbf{b}}=P\mathbf{b}.$$
$P$ is **symmetric** and **idempotent** ($P^2=P$): it carves $\mathbf{b}$ into the part
inside $S$ and the part perpendicular to it, and projecting an already-projected vector
does nothing.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The matrix $P=A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ will look, at
first, like a formula you would have to memorize. By the end it should feel forced: it is
the *unique* operator that keeps whatever already lies in $S$, annihilates whatever is
perpendicular to $S$, and does so by the only rule geometry allows — drop a perpendicular.
The phrase "least squares = projection," which the Regression track treats as its founding
idea, will turn out to be a one-line consequence of the closest-point theorem we prove in
@sec-closest.

# Why we are here: the problem that forces projection {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **when a point lies outside a subspace,
what is the most faithful stand-in for it that the subspace can offer, and why is "drop a
perpendicular" the answer?** Every derivation below is an answer to some piece of that.

## The over-determined problem, told geometrically

The historical engine is the same one that drove [REGR-01]: astronomy and geodesy around
1800. Gauss and Legendre had *more measurements than unknowns*. A surveyor measuring the
shape of the Earth, or an astronomer fixing a comet's orbit, collects dozens of noisy
readings to pin down a handful of parameters. Written as a linear system $A\mathbf{c} =
\mathbf{b}$ — where $\mathbf{c}$ holds the unknown parameters, the columns of $A$ encode
the model, and $\mathbf{b}$ holds the measurements — there are more rows (equations) than
columns (unknowns). Such a system is **over-determined**, and the ugly truth is that it
usually has **no solution at all**: no choice of $\mathbf{c}$ makes $A\mathbf{c}$ equal
$\mathbf{b}$ exactly, because the noisy measurements are mutually inconsistent.

Here is the geometric translation, and it is the whole reason this article exists. The set
of *all* vectors of the form $A\mathbf{c}$ — every linear combination of the columns of
$A$ — is a subspace, the **column space** $\mathcal{C}(A)$ (built in [GEOM-02] as the span
of the columns). Saying "$A\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{b}$ has no solution" is saying, in pictures,
that **$\mathbf{b}$ does not lie in the column space**. The measurement vector floats
*outside* the flat sheet of everything the model can produce.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why can't we just solve $A\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{b}$ — isn't that what linear algebra is
for?"* Because in the over-determined regime the equation is a lie: there is no exact
$\mathbf{c}$. You have, say, $1000$ equations and $3$ unknowns; three numbers cannot
satisfy a thousand independent constraints. The honest move is to *give up on equality*
and instead ask for the $\mathbf{c}$ that makes $A\mathbf{c}$ **as close as possible** to
$\mathbf{b}$. "As close as possible," measured by ordinary Euclidean distance, is exactly
the projection problem. Insisting on an exact solve where none exists is the beginner's
error; replacing it with "closest reachable point" is the entire idea of least squares.
:::

So we abandon exact equality and ask the next-best question: **of all points $A\mathbf{c}$
the model can reach — all points in the column space — which one is nearest to
$\mathbf{b}$?** That nearest reachable point is the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{b}$
onto $\mathcal{C}(A)$, and the leftover gap $\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{c}}$ is the part of
the data the model simply cannot explain. Minimizing the *length* of that gap is least
squares; finding the *foot of the perpendicular* is projection; and the punchline of this
article is that **they are the same thing.**

## Why "perpendicular" is the right notion of closest

Before any algebra, why should the closest point be the foot of a *perpendicular*? Picture
yourself standing at $\mathbf{b}$, off the floor, and you must touch the floor (a plane
$S$). The shortest reach is straight down — the perpendicular. Any slanted reach is longer,
because a slanted segment and the straight-down segment form a right triangle whose
*hypotenuse* is the slanted reach, and the hypotenuse is always the longest side
(Pythagoras, which lives in [GEOM-04]). The perpendicular is the one leg; everything else
is a hypotenuse. We will turn this picture into a proof in @sec-closest, but hold onto it:
**right angle = shortest distance** is not a coincidence, it is Pythagoras.

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Start in the simplest possible setting: $S$ is a single line through the origin, the set of
all scalar multiples of one vector $\mathbf{a}$. We want to write any point $\mathbf{b}$ as

$$
\mathbf{b} = \underbrace{\hat{\mathbf{b}}}_{\text{along } \mathbf{a}}
            + \underbrace{\mathbf{r}}_{\perp\, \mathbf{a}},
$$

splitting $\mathbf{b}$ into "the shadow it casts along the line" plus "the part sticking
out perpendicular." The shadow $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is some multiple $c\mathbf{a}$ of the
direction; the leftover $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{b}-c\mathbf{a}$ must be perpendicular to
$\mathbf{a}$. That one perpendicularity requirement will pin down the single number $c$.
@fig-line-intuition shows the picture that the algebra of @sec-line is going to formalize.

```{python}
#| label: fig-line-intuition
#| fig-cap: 'Projecting a point $\\mathbf{b}$ onto the line spanned by $\\mathbf{a}$. The projection $\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}=c\\mathbf{a}$ is the shadow $\\mathbf{b}$ casts along the line; the residual $\\mathbf{r}=\\mathbf{b}-\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}$ (green) joins the foot of the perpendicular to $\\mathbf{b}$ and meets the line at a right angle. The number $c$ is whatever makes that angle exactly $90^\\circ$ — nothing else.'
a = np.array([3.0, 1.0])
b = np.array([1.5, 2.6])
c = (a @ b) / (a @ a)
bhat = c * a

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.0, 4.2))
# the line spanned by a
t = np.linspace(-0.4, 1.6, 50)
ax.plot(t * a[0], t * a[1], color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--", zorder=1)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), a, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{a}$", label_offset=(0.25, -0.15))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), b, color=HILITE, label=r"$\mathbf{b}$", label_offset=(0.0, 0.25))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), bhat, color=ACCENT, lw=2.0)
ax.annotate("", xy=b, xytext=bhat,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.2, ls=":"))
ax.text(bhat[0] + 0.15, bhat[1] - 0.35, r"$\hat{\mathbf{b}}=c\mathbf{a}$",
        color=ACCENT, fontsize=12)
ax.text((bhat[0]+b[0])/2 + 0.15, (bhat[1]+b[1])/2, r"$\mathbf{r}$",
        color=ACCENT, fontsize=13)
# right-angle marker at the foot
d1 = (a / np.linalg.norm(a)) * 0.28
d2 = ((b - bhat) / np.linalg.norm(b - bhat)) * 0.28
corner = bhat
ax.plot([corner[0]+d1[0], corner[0]+d1[0]+d2[0], corner[0]+d2[0]],
        [corner[1]+d1[1], corner[1]+d1[1]+d2[1], corner[1]+d2[1]],
        color=GREY, lw=1.2)
ax.set_xlim(-0.6, 4.2); ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 3.2)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax.set_title("Projection onto a line: shadow plus perpendicular leftover")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Then we climb. Replace the line by a plane (two independent directions), then by any
subspace (a matrix's worth of directions). The split is always the same — *in the subspace*
plus *perpendicular to it* — and the requirement that pins it down is always the same — the
residual is perpendicular to **every** direction that spans the subspace. The only thing
that grows is the bookkeeping, and matrices are exactly the bookkeeping device built for it.

# Formal development I: projection onto a line {#sec-line}

We do the one-dimensional case completely, because every later case is this case repeated
once per basis direction.

## Setting up the one unknown

::: {#def-proj-line}
**Definition (projection onto a line).** Let $\mathbf{a}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ be a nonzero
vector, spanning the line $S=\{t\mathbf{a} : t\in\mathbb{R}\}$. The **orthogonal
projection** of a point $\mathbf{b}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ onto $S$ is the vector
$\hat{\mathbf{b}}=c\,\mathbf{a}$ whose residual $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{b}-c\mathbf{a}$ is
orthogonal to $\mathbf{a}$:
$$
\mathbf{a}^\top(\mathbf{b}-c\mathbf{a}) = 0.
$$ {#eq-line-orth}
:::

Why is this the right definition and not, say, "the multiple of $\mathbf{a}$ closest to
$\mathbf{b}$"? It is both — and we will *prove* they coincide in @sec-closest. For now we
take orthogonality as the defining instruction (it is the one that turns into clean
algebra) and earn the "closest" interpretation later.

## Solving for the one number

Everything is one equation, @eq-line-orth, in one unknown $c$. Expand the dot product
using its linearity — $\mathbf{a}^\top(\mathbf{u}+\mathbf{v})=\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{u}+
\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{a}^\top(c\mathbf{a})=c(\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a})$,
both established in [GEOM-04]:

$$
\mathbf{a}^\top(\mathbf{b}-c\mathbf{a})
= \mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b} - c\,\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a} = 0.
$$

Solve for $c$. Because $\mathbf{a}\neq\mathbf{0}$, its squared length
$\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}=\lVert\mathbf{a}\rVert^2$ is strictly positive (a sum of squares
that is zero only for the zero vector — [GEOM-04]), so we may divide by it:

$$
c = \frac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}.
$$ {#eq-line-c}

Therefore the projection is

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{b}}
= c\,\mathbf{a}
= \frac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}.\;}
$$ {#eq-line-proj}

Read the formula slowly, because every piece carries meaning. The scalar
$\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}$ measures how much of $\mathbf{b}$ points along $\mathbf{a}$
(it is $\lVert\mathbf{a}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{b}\rVert\cos\theta$ by the angle relation of
[GEOM-04], so it grows with the alignment of $\mathbf{b}$ to $\mathbf{a}$). Dividing by
$\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}=\lVert\mathbf{a}\rVert^2$ rescales for the *length* of
$\mathbf{a}$, so the answer does not change if we replace $\mathbf{a}$ by $2\mathbf{a}$ —
the line is the same, so the projection must be the same. Let us verify that invariance,
since it is exactly the kind of check that confirms the formula is geometric and not an
accident of scaling.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does the projection depend on how long $\mathbf{a}$ is?"* It must not — a line does not
know the length of the arrow you used to describe it. Replace $\mathbf{a}$ by $k\mathbf{a}$
for any $k\neq 0$ in @eq-line-proj:
$$
\frac{(k\mathbf{a})^\top\mathbf{b}}{(k\mathbf{a})^\top(k\mathbf{a})}\,(k\mathbf{a})
= \frac{k\,\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{k^2\,\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}\,(k\mathbf{a})
= \frac{k^2}{k^2}\cdot\frac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}
= \frac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}.
$$
The $k$'s cancel exactly: two factors upstairs (one from the dot product, one from the
trailing $\mathbf{a}$) against two downstairs. The projection depends only on the
*direction* of $\mathbf{a}$, as a geometric quantity must. If $\mathbf{a}$ is already a
unit vector ($\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}=1$), the formula collapses to
$\hat{\mathbf{b}}=(\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b})\,\mathbf{a}$ — the coordinate-as-dot-product
rule from [GEOM-05]. This is the first hint that *orthonormal* bases make projection
trivial, the theme [GEOM-07] develops.
:::

## The residual is orthogonal — by construction, and let us check it

We built $c$ precisely so that @eq-line-orth holds, so orthogonality is true by
construction. But it is worth confirming directly, because the same computation reappears
in every higher dimension. The residual is

$$
\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}}
           = \mathbf{b} - \frac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}.
$$

Take its dot product with $\mathbf{a}$:

$$
\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{r}
= \mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}
  - \frac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}
= \mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b} = 0.
$$

The scalar $\dfrac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}$ multiplied by
$\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}$ cancels the denominator, leaving $\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}$,
which subtracts off the first term. The residual is orthogonal to $\mathbf{a}$, hence to
the entire line $S$ (any point of $S$ is $t\mathbf{a}$, and
$(t\mathbf{a})^\top\mathbf{r}=t\,\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{r}=0$). The decomposition
$\mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{b}}+\mathbf{r}$ has split $\mathbf{b}$ cleanly into a part on the
line and a part perpendicular to it — exactly the orthogonal-complement decomposition
promised in [GEOM-05].

## A line projection as a matrix {#sec-line-matrix}

We can already package @eq-line-proj as a matrix, which previews the general formula. Write
$\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ regrouping the scalar:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}}
= \mathbf{a}\,\frac{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{b}}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}
= \frac{\mathbf{a}\,\mathbf{a}^\top}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}\,\mathbf{b}
= P\mathbf{b},
\qquad
P = \frac{\mathbf{a}\mathbf{a}^\top}{\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}}.
$$ {#eq-line-P}

One subtlety earns a sentence, because it is the kind of thing that trips up a rusty
reader. The numerator $\mathbf{a}\mathbf{a}^\top$ is an **outer product**: a column vector
times a row vector, which produces an $n\times n$ *matrix*, not a scalar. The denominator
$\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}$ is an **inner product**: a row times a column, a single number.
So $P$ is a matrix divided by a number — an $n\times n$ matrix. Contrast this with the
order $\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{a}$ (number) versus $\mathbf{a}\mathbf{a}^\top$ (matrix): in
matrix multiplication the *order* decides the shape, a fact developed fully in [GEOM-09].
We will see in @sec-P-properties that this $P$ is symmetric and idempotent, the
fingerprints of every projection matrix.

# Formal development II: projection onto a subspace {#sec-subspace}

Now the real thing. Replace the single direction $\mathbf{a}$ by a whole subspace $S$,
described as the column space of a matrix.

## The setup, and the one geometric demand {#sec-setup}

Let $S\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ be a subspace spanned by $k$ vectors
$\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_k$, which we **assume linearly independent** (no redundancy;
in the rank-deficient case the formula below needs the pseudoinverse — see @sec-rank-def
and [GEOM-21]). Stack them as the columns of an $n\times k$ matrix

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a}_1 & \mathbf{a}_2 & \cdots & \mathbf{a}_k \end{bmatrix}.
$$

Then the subspace $S$ *is* the column space $\mathcal{C}(A)$: every point of $S$ is a
linear combination $c_1\mathbf{a}_1+\cdots+c_k\mathbf{a}_k$, and stacking the coefficients
into $\mathbf{c}=(c_1,\dots,c_k)^\top$, that combination is exactly the matrix–vector
product $A\mathbf{c}$ — "$A$ times $\mathbf{c}$ is a weighted sum of the columns of $A$"
(this reading of $A\mathbf{c}$ is the central fact of [GEOM-08]; here we just use it).

So the projection $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$, being a point of $S$, equals $A\hat{\mathbf{c}}$ for
some coefficient vector $\hat{\mathbf{c}}\in\mathbb{R}^k$ that we must find. The residual is

$$
\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{b} - \hat{\mathbf{b}} = \mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{c}}.
$$

The single geometric demand — the entire content of "orthogonal projection" — is that
**the residual is perpendicular to the whole subspace $S$.** A residual is perpendicular to
all of $S$ exactly when it is perpendicular to each spanning vector, because if
$\mathbf{r}\perp\mathbf{a}_j$ for every $j$, then for any combination
$\mathbf{s}=\sum_j c_j\mathbf{a}_j\in S$ we get $\mathbf{s}^\top\mathbf{r}=\sum_j
c_j(\mathbf{a}_j^\top\mathbf{r})=\sum_j c_j\cdot 0 = 0$. So the demand is the $k$
conditions

$$
\mathbf{a}_j^\top(\mathbf{b}-A\hat{\mathbf{c}}) = 0,\qquad j=1,\dots,k.
$$ {#eq-k-conditions}

## Stacking the $k$ conditions into the normal equations {#sec-normal}

We have $k$ scalar equations, @eq-k-conditions, one per column. Stacking $k$ scalar
equations is exactly what a matrix product does, and recognizing this is the crux. The row
vector $\mathbf{a}_j^\top$ is the $j$-th *row* of $A^\top$ (transposing $A$ turns its
columns into rows — [GEOM-10]). So stacking the $k$ numbers
$\mathbf{a}_1^\top\mathbf{r},\dots,\mathbf{a}_k^\top\mathbf{r}$ into a column is precisely
the matrix–vector product $A^\top\mathbf{r}$:

$$
A^\top\mathbf{r}
= \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a}_1^\top\mathbf{r} \\ \vdots \\ \mathbf{a}_k^\top\mathbf{r} \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ \vdots \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
= \mathbf{0}.
$$

So the $k$ orthogonality conditions @eq-k-conditions collapse into one tidy matrix
equation, the geometric heart of everything:

$$
A^\top(\mathbf{b} - A\hat{\mathbf{c}}) = \mathbf{0}.
$$ {#eq-normal-geom}

Read it in English first: *$A^\top$ times the residual is zero* — the residual is
orthogonal to every column of $A$. Now expand it. Distribute $A^\top$ across the
subtraction ($A^\top(\mathbf{u}-\mathbf{v})=A^\top\mathbf{u}-A^\top\mathbf{v}$, the
linearity of matrix multiplication — [GEOM-08]):

$$
A^\top\mathbf{b} - A^\top A\,\hat{\mathbf{c}} = \mathbf{0},
$$

and move the second term to the other side:

$$
\boxed{\;A^\top A\,\hat{\mathbf{c}} = A^\top\mathbf{b}.\;}
$$ {#eq-normal}

These are the **normal equations**. The word *normal* means *perpendicular*, not ordinary
— it records that we built them by demanding the residual be normal (at right angles) to
the column space. They are a square $k\times k$ linear system: $A^\top A$ is $k\times k$
(a $k\times n$ matrix times an $n\times k$ matrix), and $A^\top\mathbf{b}$ is a length-$k$
column. We have converted an unsolvable $n\times k$ over-determined system
$A\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{b}$ into a solvable $k\times k$ square one — *that* is the payoff of
projecting.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one breath, where do the normal equations come from?"* From a single sentence:
**the residual is orthogonal to every column of $A$.** Written out, "orthogonal to every
column" is "$A^\top(\mathbf{b}-A\hat{\mathbf{c}})=\mathbf{0}$," which rearranges to $A^\top
A\hat{\mathbf{c}}=A^\top\mathbf{b}$. If you can say that sentence and turn it into that
equation on a whiteboard, you have demonstrated that you understand least squares
geometrically and not as a memorized formula. This is the [REGR-04]/[REGR-05] content in its
purest, statistics-free form: there $\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{y}$ (the response), $A=X$ (the
design matrix), and @eq-normal is $X^\top X\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}=X^\top\mathbf{y}$.
:::

## Solving them: when is $A^\top A$ invertible? {#sec-invertible}

To solve @eq-normal for $\hat{\mathbf{c}}$ we want to invert $A^\top A$. We cannot assert
this is possible — we must earn it. The claim is that **$A^\top A$ is invertible exactly
when the columns of $A$ are linearly independent**, which is the assumption we made in
@sec-setup. We prove the direction we need (independent columns $\Rightarrow$ invertible);
the converse is in @sec-rank-def.

::: {#lem-AtA}
**Lemma.** If the columns of $A$ are linearly independent, then the $k\times k$ matrix
$A^\top A$ is invertible.
:::

::: {.proof}
A square matrix is invertible if and only if the only vector it sends to zero is the zero
vector — i.e. its null space is trivial (this characterization is established in [GEOM-11];
geometrically, a map with a trivial null space crushes nothing, so it can be undone). So we
show: if $A^\top A\,\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$, then $\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$.

Suppose $A^\top A\,\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$. The trick is to multiply on the left by
$\mathbf{v}^\top$, turning the matrix equation into a statement about a *length*:

$$
\mathbf{v}^\top A^\top A\,\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{v}^\top\mathbf{0} = 0.
$$

Now read the left side as a length. Group it as $(A\mathbf{v})^\top(A\mathbf{v})$ — legal
because $\mathbf{v}^\top A^\top = (A\mathbf{v})^\top$ (the transpose of a product reverses
order, $(A\mathbf{v})^\top=\mathbf{v}^\top A^\top$, from [GEOM-10]). So

$$
(A\mathbf{v})^\top(A\mathbf{v}) = \lVert A\mathbf{v}\rVert^2 = 0.
$$

A squared length is zero only when the vector itself is zero ([GEOM-04]), so
$A\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$. But $A\mathbf{v}$ is the linear combination
$v_1\mathbf{a}_1+\cdots+v_k\mathbf{a}_k$ of the columns of $A$. Linear independence of the
columns means the *only* combination equal to zero is the one with all coefficients zero
([GEOM-03]). Hence $\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$. The null space of $A^\top A$ is therefore
trivial, so $A^\top A$ is invertible. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

With invertibility secured, we solve @eq-normal by left-multiplying by $(A^\top A)^{-1}$:

$$
\hat{\mathbf{c}} = (A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top\mathbf{b}.
$$ {#eq-chat}

And the projection itself, $\hat{\mathbf{b}}=A\hat{\mathbf{c}}$, is

$$
\boxed{\;\hat{\mathbf{b}} = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top\,\mathbf{b} = P\mathbf{b},
\qquad P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top.\;}
$$ {#eq-P}

This is the **projection matrix**. We can record the central result formally.

::: {#thm-projection}
**Theorem (orthogonal projection onto a column space).** Let $A$ be an $n\times k$ matrix
with linearly independent columns spanning the subspace $S=\mathcal{C}(A)$. For any
$\mathbf{b}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, there is a unique point $\hat{\mathbf{b}}\in S$ whose residual
$\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is orthogonal to $S$, namely
$$
\hat{\mathbf{b}} = P\mathbf{b},\qquad P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top,
$$
and $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is the **closest point of $S$ to $\mathbf{b}$** in Euclidean
distance.
:::

::: {.proof}
*Existence and the formula:* @sec-setup through @sec-invertible. The orthogonality demand
@eq-normal-geom is equivalent to the normal equations @eq-normal; @lem-AtA makes $A^\top A$
invertible under the independence hypothesis, so @eq-chat gives a coefficient vector
$\hat{\mathbf{c}}$ and hence the point $\hat{\mathbf{b}}=A\hat{\mathbf{c}}=P\mathbf{b}$,
whose residual is orthogonal to $S$ by construction.

*Uniqueness:* suppose two points $\hat{\mathbf{b}}_1,\hat{\mathbf{b}}_2\in S$ both have
residuals orthogonal to $S$. Their difference
$\mathbf{d}=\hat{\mathbf{b}}_1-\hat{\mathbf{b}}_2$ lies in $S$ (a subspace is closed under
subtraction — [GEOM-02]). It also equals the difference of the two residuals,
$(\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}_2)-(\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}_1)$, a difference of two
vectors each orthogonal to $S$, hence itself orthogonal to $S$. So $\mathbf{d}$ is in $S$
*and* orthogonal to $S$, which forces $\mathbf{d}^\top\mathbf{d}=0$ (a vector orthogonal to
itself has zero length), so $\mathbf{d}=\mathbf{0}$ and the two points coincide.

*Closest point:* proved separately in @sec-closest. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Do not literally form $(A^\top A)^{-1}$ and multiply it out.** The formula
$P=A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ is the right object to *reason* with, but it is the wrong thing
to *compute* with. Two reasons. First, explicitly inverting a matrix is slower and less
accurate than solving the linear system @eq-normal directly — "solve, don't invert" is a
rule we make precise in [GEOM-26]. Second, and worse, forming $A^\top A$ **squares the
condition number** of the problem: if the columns of $A$ are nearly dependent (near
collinear), $A^\top A$ is dangerously close to singular and the answer dissolves into
floating-point noise. The professional route is a **QR** factorization ([GEOM-07]) or the
**SVD** ([GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21]), which project without ever forming $A^\top A$. The normal
equations are the cleanest *derivation* and a fine computation on well-behaved data; they
are not the algorithm you ship.
:::

# The projection matrix is symmetric and idempotent {#sec-P-properties}

The matrix $P$ has two algebraic fingerprints that *are* its geometry. We prove both, then
read off what each means. Throughout, $P=A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$, and we abbreviate
$M=A^\top A$ (a symmetric, invertible $k\times k$ matrix).

We need two transpose facts, both from [GEOM-10] and both restated here so nothing is
implicit: $(XY)^\top = Y^\top X^\top$ (transpose reverses a product) and
$(X^{-1})^\top = (X^\top)^{-1}$ (transpose and inverse commute). We also need that
$M=A^\top A$ is symmetric, i.e. $M^\top=M$: indeed $M^\top=(A^\top A)^\top = A^\top
(A^\top)^\top = A^\top A = M$, using $(A^\top)^\top=A$.

## Symmetry: $P^\top = P$

::: {#lem-sym}
**Lemma.** $P^\top = P$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Transpose $P=A\,M^{-1}A^\top$ by reversing the order of the three factors and transposing
each:

$$
P^\top
= \bigl(A\,M^{-1}A^\top\bigr)^\top
= (A^\top)^\top\,(M^{-1})^\top\,A^\top
= A\,(M^{-1})^\top\,A^\top.
$$

Now $(M^{-1})^\top = (M^\top)^{-1}$, and since $M$ is symmetric ($M^\top=M$) this is just
$M^{-1}$. Substituting,

$$
P^\top = A\,M^{-1}A^\top = P. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

What does symmetry *mean* geometrically? It is the statement that projection respects the
dot product evenhandedly: $(P\mathbf{x})^\top\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{x}^\top(P\mathbf{y})$ for
all $\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}$ (because $(P\mathbf{x})^\top\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{x}^\top
P^\top\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{x}^\top P\mathbf{y}$). An *oblique* projection — one that drops to
the subspace along some slanted direction instead of straight down — would still be
idempotent but would **not** be symmetric. Symmetry is exactly the algebraic signature of
the angle being a *right* angle. This matters: in [REGR-11] (weighted/generalized least
squares) the relevant projection is oblique with respect to the ordinary dot product, and
losing symmetry is the visible sign that the geometry has changed.

## Idempotence: $P^2 = P$

::: {#lem-idem}
**Lemma.** $P^2 = P$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Multiply $P$ by itself and watch the middle collapse:

$$
P^2 = \bigl(A\,M^{-1}A^\top\bigr)\bigl(A\,M^{-1}A^\top\bigr)
    = A\,M^{-1}\,\underbrace{(A^\top A)}_{=\,M}\,M^{-1}A^\top.
$$

The middle three factors are $M^{-1}\,M\,M^{-1}$. Since $M^{-1}M=I$ (the identity, by
definition of the inverse), this is $I\,M^{-1}=M^{-1}$. Hence

$$
P^2 = A\,M^{-1}A^\top = P. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Idempotence is the cleanest geometric statement in the article: **projecting a vector that
already lies in the subspace does nothing.** If $\mathbf{x}\in S$ then $\mathbf{x}$ is its
own closest point in $S$, so $P\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{x}$; and any output $P\mathbf{b}$ already
lies in $S$, so applying $P$ a second time returns it unchanged — that is precisely
$P(P\mathbf{b})=P\mathbf{b}$, i.e. $P^2=P$. Conversely, on the perpendicular part: a vector
$\mathbf{w}$ orthogonal to $S$ projects to $\mathbf{0}$ (its closest point in $S$ is the
origin). So $P$ keeps the in-subspace part of $\mathbf{b}$ and deletes the perpendicular
part — it *is* the decomposition $\mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{b}}+\mathbf{r}$ rendered as an
operator.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What are the eigenvalues of a projection matrix?"* Only $0$ and $1$ — and you can see why
without computing a characteristic polynomial. From $P^2=P$, any eigenvalue $\lambda$ with
eigenvector $\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}$ satisfies $P\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$, so
$P^2\mathbf{v}=\lambda^2\mathbf{v}$; but $P^2=P$ means $P^2\mathbf{v}=P\mathbf{v}=
\lambda\mathbf{v}$, hence $\lambda^2\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$, i.e.
$(\lambda^2-\lambda)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$. Since $\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}$ we need
$\lambda^2=\lambda$, so $\lambda\in\{0,1\}$. The eigenvectors with $\lambda=1$ are the
vectors already in $S$ (projection leaves them fixed); those with $\lambda=0$ are the
vectors perpendicular to $S$ (projection kills them). The number of $1$'s — the rank of
$P$, also its **trace** — equals $\dim S = k$. This is the eigen-picture the spectral
theorem ([GEOM-16]) and the SVD ([GEOM-20]) will reuse.
:::

## The complementary projection $I - P$

If $P$ keeps the part of $\mathbf{b}$ inside $S$, then $I-P$ keeps the part perpendicular
to $S$ — the residual operator. Indeed $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}=
\mathbf{b}-P\mathbf{b}=(I-P)\mathbf{b}$. It is itself a projection: it is symmetric
($(I-P)^\top=I-P^\top=I-P$) and idempotent ($(I-P)^2 = I-2P+P^2 = I-2P+P = I-P$, using
$P^2=P$). It projects onto the **orthogonal complement** $S^\perp$ — everything
perpendicular to $S$ ([GEOM-05]). The pair $(P,\,I-P)$ splits all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ into
$S\oplus S^\perp$, the orthogonal decomposition that underlies the Pythagorean accounting
of the next section.

# The foot of the perpendicular really is the closest point {#sec-closest}

We have been *calling* $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ the closest point; now we prove it. This is the
clause of @thm-projection we deferred, and it is the bridge to "least squares = minimize
distance."

::: {#thm-closest}
**Theorem (best approximation).** Let $\hat{\mathbf{b}}=P\mathbf{b}$ be the orthogonal
projection of $\mathbf{b}$ onto $S$. Then for *every other* point $\mathbf{s}\in S$,
$$
\lVert \mathbf{b}-\mathbf{s}\rVert \;>\; \lVert\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}\rVert.
$$
The projection is strictly the nearest point of $S$ to $\mathbf{b}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Pick any $\mathbf{s}\in S$ with $\mathbf{s}\neq\hat{\mathbf{b}}$. Split the gap
$\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{s}$ by inserting and removing $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$:

$$
\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{s}
= \underbrace{(\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}})}_{=\,\mathbf{r}}
+ \underbrace{(\hat{\mathbf{b}}-\mathbf{s})}_{=\,\mathbf{d}}.
$$

Examine the two pieces. The first, $\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}$, is the
residual — orthogonal to all of $S$ by construction. The second,
$\mathbf{d}=\hat{\mathbf{b}}-\mathbf{s}$, is a difference of two points of $S$, hence lies
*in* $S$ (subspaces are closed under subtraction — [GEOM-02]). Therefore $\mathbf{r}$ and
$\mathbf{d}$ are **orthogonal** to each other: $\mathbf{r}^\top\mathbf{d}=0$, because
$\mathbf{r}$ is perpendicular to everything in $S$ and $\mathbf{d}\in S$.

Now take the squared length of the sum and use the **Pythagorean theorem in $n$
dimensions** ([GEOM-05]): when two vectors are orthogonal, the squared length of their sum
is the sum of their squared lengths. (For completeness, the one-line reason:
$\lVert\mathbf{r}+\mathbf{d}\rVert^2 = (\mathbf{r}+\mathbf{d})^\top(\mathbf{r}+\mathbf{d})
= \mathbf{r}^\top\mathbf{r} + 2\mathbf{r}^\top\mathbf{d} + \mathbf{d}^\top\mathbf{d}$, and
the cross term $2\mathbf{r}^\top\mathbf{d}$ vanishes by orthogonality.) Hence

$$
\lVert \mathbf{b}-\mathbf{s}\rVert^2
= \lVert\mathbf{r}\rVert^2 + \lVert\mathbf{d}\rVert^2
= \lVert\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}\rVert^2 + \lVert\hat{\mathbf{b}}-\mathbf{s}\rVert^2.
$$

Since $\mathbf{s}\neq\hat{\mathbf{b}}$, the term $\lVert\hat{\mathbf{b}}-\mathbf{s}\rVert^2$
is strictly positive, so

$$
\lVert \mathbf{b}-\mathbf{s}\rVert^2
> \lVert\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}\rVert^2,
$$

and taking square roots (a strictly increasing operation on nonnegative numbers) gives the
claim. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This proof is the keystone of the keystone. Notice what it says: the squared distance from
$\mathbf{b}$ to *any* trial point $\mathbf{s}$ splits, by Pythagoras, into a fixed piece
(the residual length, which no choice of $\mathbf{s}$ can shrink) plus a movable piece (the
in-subspace distance $\lVert\hat{\mathbf{b}}-\mathbf{s}\rVert^2$, which is minimized — to
zero — only by taking $\mathbf{s}=\hat{\mathbf{b}}$). Choosing the perpendicular foot
zeroes out the only part you control. **That is why minimizing squared distance and
dropping a perpendicular are the same act.** @fig-pythagoras draws this right triangle.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pythagoras
#| fig-cap: 'The closest-point theorem is the Pythagorean theorem. For any trial point $\\mathbf{s}$ in the subspace $S$ (here a line), the gap $\\mathbf{b}-\\mathbf{s}$ is the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose legs are the residual $\\mathbf{r}=\\mathbf{b}-\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}$ (perpendicular to $S$) and the in-subspace displacement $\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}-\\mathbf{s}$. The hypotenuse is longest exactly when $\\mathbf{s}\\ne\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}$; it is shortest, equal to the leg $\\mathbf{r}$, only at $\\mathbf{s}=\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}$.'
a = np.array([3.0, 0.6])
b = np.array([1.2, 2.7])
bhat = (a @ b)/(a @ a) * a
s = bhat - 0.55 * a / np.linalg.norm(a)   # another point on the line

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.2, 4.2))
t = np.linspace(-0.3, 1.5, 50)
ax.plot(t*a[0], t*a[1], color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--", zorder=1)
ax.text(1.5*a[0]-0.2, 1.5*a[1]+0.1, r"$S$", color=GREY, fontsize=13)
ax.scatter(*b, color=HILITE, s=60, zorder=5)
ax.text(b[0]-0.05, b[1]+0.22, r"$\mathbf{b}$", color=HILITE, fontsize=13)
ax.scatter(*bhat, color=ACCENT, s=55, zorder=5)
ax.text(bhat[0]+0.05, bhat[1]-0.32, r"$\hat{\mathbf{b}}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=13)
ax.scatter(*s, color=VEC, s=55, zorder=5)
ax.text(s[0]-0.15, s[1]-0.32, r"$\mathbf{s}$", color=VEC, fontsize=13)
# triangle
ax.plot([b[0], bhat[0]], [b[1], bhat[1]], color=ACCENT, lw=2.4)   # residual leg
ax.plot([bhat[0], s[0]], [bhat[1], s[1]], color=VEC, lw=2.4)      # in-subspace leg
ax.plot([b[0], s[0]], [b[1], s[1]], color=HILITE, lw=2.4, ls=":") # hypotenuse
ax.text((b[0]+bhat[0])/2+0.12, (b[1]+bhat[1])/2, r"$\mathbf{r}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=13)
ax.text((b[0]+s[0])/2-0.25, (b[1]+s[1])/2+0.1, r"$\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{s}$", color=HILITE, fontsize=11)
# right angle marker at bhat
d1 = (a/np.linalg.norm(a))*0.22; d2 = ((b-bhat)/np.linalg.norm(b-bhat))*0.22
ax.plot([bhat[0]+d1[0], bhat[0]+d1[0]+d2[0], bhat[0]+d2[0]],
        [bhat[1]+d1[1], bhat[1]+d1[1]+d2[1], bhat[1]+d2[1]], color=GREY, lw=1.1)
ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 4.5); ax.set_ylim(-0.4, 3.2)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$")
ax.set_title("Right angle $\\Rightarrow$ shortest distance, by Pythagoras")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the closest-point problem a calculus problem or a geometry problem?"* Both, and seeing
the equivalence is the point. The calculus route minimizes
$f(\mathbf{c})=\lVert\mathbf{b}-A\mathbf{c}\rVert^2$ by setting its gradient to zero; the
gradient is $\nabla f = 2A^\top(A\mathbf{c}-\mathbf{b})$, and $\nabla f=\mathbf{0}$ is
*exactly* the normal equations @eq-normal (this gradient is derived in full in [GEOM-25],
and it is the same computation that [REGR-01] does by hand for one predictor). The geometry
route — @thm-closest — never differentiates anything; it splits a right triangle and
invokes Pythagoras. The two give the identical system because "the directional derivative
of squared distance is zero in every in-subspace direction" *is* "the residual is
perpendicular to every in-subspace direction." Calculus and geometry are describing one
condition in two dialects.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

Three examples, escalating in dimension and honesty. The first we verify by hand; the last
sits at the edge of applicability, where projection strains.

## Example 1 — projecting onto a plane in $\mathbb{R}^3$ {#sec-ex-plane}

Take the plane $S$ spanned by $\mathbf{a}_1=(1,0,1)^\top$ and $\mathbf{a}_2=(0,1,1)^\top$,
and the external point $\mathbf{b}=(2,3,0)^\top$. The two spanning vectors are independent
(neither is a multiple of the other), so @thm-projection applies. We find the closest point
of the plane to $\mathbf{b}$.

```{python}
#| label: ex-plane
A = np.array([[1.0, 0.0],
              [0.0, 1.0],
              [1.0, 1.0]])
b = np.array([2.0, 3.0, 0.0])

AtA = A.T @ A
Atb = A.T @ b
chat = np.linalg.solve(AtA, Atb)     # solve the normal equations, don't invert
bhat = A @ chat
r = b - bhat

print("A^T A =\n", AtA)
print("A^T b =", Atb)
print("chat (coefficients) =", chat)
print("bhat (projection)   =", bhat)
print("residual r          =", r)
print("check A^T r = 0 ?    =", A.T @ r, " (orthogonal to both columns)")
print("dist to b           =", np.linalg.norm(r))
```

Walk the arithmetic by hand to confirm the code is doing exactly @sec-normal. First
$A^\top A$: its entries are the pairwise dot products of the columns. $\mathbf{a}_1^\top
\mathbf{a}_1 = 1+0+1 = 2$, $\mathbf{a}_1^\top\mathbf{a}_2 = 0+0+1 = 1$, and
$\mathbf{a}_2^\top\mathbf{a}_2 = 0+1+1 = 2$, so

$$
A^\top A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix},
\qquad
A^\top\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{a}_1^\top\mathbf{b} \\ \mathbf{a}_2^\top\mathbf{b}\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2+0+0 \\ 0+3+0 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

The normal equations @eq-normal are $\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\1&2\end{bmatrix}\hat{\mathbf{c}}
= \begin{bmatrix}2\\3\end{bmatrix}$. Invert the $2\times 2$ (its determinant is
$2\cdot2-1\cdot1=3$):

$$
\hat{\mathbf{c}}
= \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix}2&-1\\-1&2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2\\3\end{bmatrix}
= \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix}4-3\\-2+6\end{bmatrix}
= \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix}1\\4\end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix}1/3\\4/3\end{bmatrix},
$$

matching the printed `chat`. The projection is
$\hat{\mathbf{b}}=A\hat{\mathbf{c}}=\tfrac13\mathbf{a}_1+\tfrac43\mathbf{a}_2
=(\tfrac13,\,\tfrac43,\,\tfrac53)^\top$, and the residual is
$\mathbf{b}-\hat{\mathbf{b}}=(\tfrac53,\tfrac53,-\tfrac53)^\top$. The printed check
$A^\top\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{0}$ confirms the residual is perpendicular to *both* spanning
directions — the geometric heart of @sec-normal made numerical. @fig-plane3d draws it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-plane3d
#| fig-cap: 'Projection onto a plane in $\\mathbb{R}^3$. The point $\\mathbf{b}$ (red) sits off the plane $S=\\mathrm{span}(\\mathbf{a}_1,\\mathbf{a}_2)$. Its projection $\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}$ (green) is the foot of the perpendicular; the residual $\\mathbf{r}$ (green dotted) meets the plane at a right angle and is orthogonal to every direction lying in it. Of all points on the plane, $\\hat{\\mathbf{b}}$ is the one nearest $\\mathbf{b}$.'
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.6, 5.0))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
# plane mesh from the two spanning vectors
ss = np.linspace(-0.6, 2.2, 12); tt = np.linspace(-0.4, 2.0, 12)
SS, TT = np.meshgrid(ss, tt)
PX = SS*A[0,0] + TT*A[0,1]
PY = SS*A[1,0] + TT*A[1,1]
PZ = SS*A[2,0] + TT*A[2,1]
ax.plot_surface(PX, PY, PZ, alpha=0.25, color=PALE, linewidth=0)
# spanning vectors
for col, name, off in [(A[:,0], r"$\mathbf{a}_1$", 0.1), (A[:,1], r"$\mathbf{a}_2$", 0.1)]:
    ax.quiver(0,0,0, *col, color=VEC, lw=2.0, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
    ax.text(col[0], col[1], col[2]+off, name, color=VEC, fontsize=12)
# b, bhat, residual
ax.quiver(0,0,0, *b, color=HILITE, lw=2.4, arrow_length_ratio=0.10)
ax.text(b[0], b[1], b[2]+0.15, r"$\mathbf{b}$", color=HILITE, fontsize=13)
ax.scatter(*bhat, color=ACCENT, s=55)
ax.text(bhat[0], bhat[1], bhat[2]-0.45, r"$\hat{\mathbf{b}}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=13)
ax.plot([b[0],bhat[0]],[b[1],bhat[1]],[b[2],bhat[2]], color=ACCENT, lw=2.2, ls=":")
ax.set_xlabel("$x_1$"); ax.set_ylabel("$x_2$"); ax.set_zlabel("$x_3$")
ax.set_title("Dropping a perpendicular onto a plane")
ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=-60)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — least squares is projection: fitting a line through data {#sec-ex-leastsq}

This is the example the whole track is built to reach. We have $n$ data points $(t_i,y_i)$
and want the best straight line $y\approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 t$. In the language of this
article, that is asking for the projection of the response vector
$\mathbf{y}=(y_1,\dots,y_n)^\top$ onto the column space of the **design matrix**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & t_1 \\ 1 & t_2 \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ 1 & t_n \end{bmatrix},
\qquad \mathbf{c} = \begin{bmatrix}\beta_0\\\beta_1\end{bmatrix},
$$

because $A\mathbf{c}$ is the vector of fitted values $\beta_0 + \beta_1 t_i$. There is no
$\mathbf{c}$ making $A\mathbf{c}=\mathbf{y}$ exactly — the points are not collinear, so
$\mathbf{y}$ lies *outside* the (two-dimensional) column space — and the projection finds
the closest reachable fitted-value vector. We use a real, mildly noisy relationship: the
amount of stretch in a spring versus the load hung on it (Hooke's-law territory), where the
linear model is genuinely appropriate.

```{python}
#| label: fig-leastsq
#| fig-cap: 'Least squares **is** projection. **Left:** load–extension data for a spring with the OLS line $\\hat y=\\hat\\beta_0+\\hat\\beta_1 t$, computed by solving the normal equations $A^\\top A\\hat{\\mathbf{c}}=A^\\top\\mathbf{y}$. **Right:** the residuals $y_i-\\hat y_i$. Their plain sum is zero (column $\\mathbf 1$ of $A$) and their $t$-weighted sum is zero (column $\\mathbf t$): that is $A^\\top(\\mathbf{y}-A\\hat{\\mathbf{c}})=\\mathbf 0$, the residual orthogonal to both columns. The fit is the projection of $\\mathbf y$ onto the plane spanned by $\\mathbf 1$ and $\\mathbf t$.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
t = np.array([0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0])   # load (N)
y = 0.8 + 1.7 * t + rng.normal(0, 0.35, size=t.size)               # extension (cm)

A = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(t), t])     # design matrix [1, t]
chat = np.linalg.solve(A.T @ A, A.T @ y)       # normal equations
yhat = A @ chat
resid = y - yhat

ts = np.linspace(t.min()-0.3, t.max()+0.3, 100)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
axL.scatter(t, y, color=VEC, s=42, zorder=3, label="data $(t_i,y_i)$")
axL.plot(ts, chat[0] + chat[1]*ts, color=HILITE,
         label=fr"$\hat y={chat[0]:.2f}+{chat[1]:.2f}\,t$")
for ti, yi, hi in zip(t, y, yhat):
    axL.plot([ti, ti], [yi, hi], color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axL.set_xlabel("load $t$ (N)"); axL.set_ylabel("extension $y$ (cm)")
axL.set_title(r"Fit = projection of $y$ onto span$(\mathbf{1},\mathbf{t})$")
axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.scatter(t, resid, color=VEC, s=42, zorder=3)
for ti, ri in zip(t, resid):
    axR.plot([ti, ti], [0, ri], color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.set_xlabel("load $t$ (N)"); axR.set_ylabel("residual")
axR.set_title("Residuals: orthogonal to both columns")
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"chat = [beta0, beta1] = {chat}")
print(f"sum of residuals          = {resid.sum(): .3e}   (= 1^T r, col of ones)")
print(f"sum of t_i * residual_i   = {np.sum(t*resid): .3e}   (= t^T r, col t)")
print(f"||residual|| (fit error)  = {np.linalg.norm(resid):.4f}")
```

The two printed checks are the normal equations made flesh. The first column of $A$ is the
all-ones vector $\mathbf{1}$, and $\mathbf{1}^\top\mathbf{r}=\sum_i r_i$ — its being zero
says the residuals balance, which is the first normal equation of [REGR-01]. The second
column is $\mathbf{t}$, and $\mathbf{t}^\top\mathbf{r}=\sum_i t_i r_i = 0$ is the second.
Together they are $A^\top\mathbf{r}=\mathbf{0}$ — the residual orthogonal to the column
space. **This is the entire content of [REGR-04 *Regression as Projection*].** The vertical
segments in the left panel are residuals; their squared lengths sum to
$\lVert\mathbf{r}\rVert^2$, which @thm-closest guarantees is the smallest achievable. The
one-predictor closed forms of [REGR-01] ($\hat\beta_1=S_{ty}/S_{tt}$, etc.) are exactly the
solution of this $2\times2$ system — the same answer, derived by calculus there and by
geometry here.

## Example 3 (edge of applicability) — near-collinear columns and the limits of the normal equations {#sec-ex-collinear}

Here is the honest boundary. The formula $P=A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ is exact when the
columns of $A$ are independent, but it degrades — badly — when they are *nearly* dependent,
and dissolves entirely when they are exactly dependent. We build a design matrix whose two
predictors are almost the same direction (an extreme but common situation: two features
that measure nearly the same thing — height in inches and height in centimeters, say), and
watch the geometry strain.

```{python}
#| label: fig-collinear
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability. Two predictor columns that point in almost the same direction (angle $\\approx 0.6^\\circ$). The column space they span is a thin sliver of a plane; projecting onto it is geometrically well-defined, but recovering the *coefficients* $\\hat{\\mathbf c}$ is wildly unstable. The bar chart shows how a tiny perturbation of $\\mathbf b$ (relative size $10^{-3}$) swings the recovered coefficients enormously, even though the projection $\\hat{\\mathbf b}$ itself barely moves. This is why the normal equations are the wrong *algorithm* near collinearity.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
n = 50
a1 = rng.normal(size=n)
a1 /= np.linalg.norm(a1)
# a2 almost equal to a1, tiny independent component
perp = rng.normal(size=n); perp -= (perp @ a1) * a1; perp /= np.linalg.norm(perp)
eps = 0.01
a2 = a1 + eps * perp
a2 /= np.linalg.norm(a2)
A = np.column_stack([a1, a2])

b = a1 * 1.0 + a2 * 0.5 + 0.3 * perp + 0.05 * rng.normal(size=n)
db = 1e-3 * rng.normal(size=n)              # tiny perturbation of b

c0 = np.linalg.solve(A.T @ A, A.T @ b)
c1 = np.linalg.solve(A.T @ A, A.T @ (b + db))
bhat0 = A @ c0; bhat1 = A @ c1

angle = np.degrees(np.arccos(np.clip(a1 @ a2, -1, 1)))
kappa = np.linalg.cond(A.T @ A)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
idx = np.arange(2)
ax.bar(idx-0.18, c0, width=0.36, color=VEC, label=r"coefficients for $\mathbf{b}$")
ax.bar(idx+0.18, c1, width=0.36, color=HILITE,
       label=r"coefficients for $\mathbf{b}+\delta\mathbf{b}$")
ax.set_xticks(idx); ax.set_xticklabels([r"$\hat c_1$", r"$\hat c_2$"])
ax.set_ylabel("recovered coefficient")
ax.set_title(f"Tiny data change, huge coefficient swing (angle $\\approx${angle:.1f}$^\\circ$)")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"angle between columns      = {angle:.3f} degrees")
print(f"cond(A^T A)                = {kappa:.3e}")
print(f"coeffs for b               = {c0}")
print(f"coeffs for b + db          = {c1}   (delta b ~ 1e-3)")
print(f"||change in coeffs||       = {np.linalg.norm(c1-c0):.4f}")
print(f"||change in projection||   = {np.linalg.norm(bhat1-bhat0):.4e}  (barely moves!)")
```

Read the contrast carefully, because it separates "knows the formula" from "knows when it
fails." The **projection itself is fine**: $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ barely moves when we jiggle
$\mathbf{b}$, because projecting onto a subspace is a geometrically stable operation
($\lVert P\rVert = 1$ — projection never magnifies). What is *not* fine is recovering the
**coefficients** $\hat{\mathbf{c}}$: because the two columns nearly coincide, many very
different coefficient vectors produce almost the same point in the column space, so a tiny
change in $\mathbf{b}$ flings the coefficients around. The culprit is the condition number
of $A^\top A$ (printed above, enormous), and forming $A^\top A$ *squares* the conditioning
of $A$ — the precise warning of the Pitfall in @sec-invertible.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Collinearity does not break the projection — it breaks the coefficients.** When features
are nearly redundant, the closest point $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ (the *prediction*) remains well
determined, but the weights $\hat{\mathbf{c}}$ that produce it (the *interpretation*)
become unreliable, sign-flippable, and explosively sensitive. If you only need predictions,
you may not care; if you need to *interpret* the coefficients, you are in trouble. This is
exactly the multicollinearity story of [REGR-07], and the remedies live downstream: drop or
combine redundant columns; use **ridge** regression ($A^\top A+\lambda I$, which un-squashes
the smallest eigenvalues — [REGR-14]); or solve via **QR**/**SVD** ([GEOM-07], [GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21])
and use the **pseudoinverse** (@sec-rank-def) to pick the minimum-norm coefficient among the
many that fit equally well.
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

@thm-projection carries one structural assumption — independent columns — and a couple of
silent ones. We make all of them explicit.

## Rank-deficient $A$: when $A^\top A$ is not invertible {#sec-rank-def}

If the columns of $A$ are linearly *dependent* — some column is a combination of the others
— then by the contrapositive of @lem-AtA, $A^\top A$ is **singular**, the inverse in
@eq-P does not exist, and the normal equations @eq-normal have *infinitely many* solutions
$\hat{\mathbf{c}}$. (Concretely: if $A\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ for some
$\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}$, then $\hat{\mathbf{c}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{c}}+\mathbf{v}$ give
the same fitted point, since $A(\hat{\mathbf{c}}+\mathbf{v})=A\hat{\mathbf{c}}$.)

But notice what does *not* fail: the **projection** $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$ is still unique. The
closest point of the subspace to $\mathbf{b}$ does not care how we coordinatize the
subspace; the *point* is determined, only its *coordinates* are not. The clean fix is to
choose, among all coefficient vectors producing $\hat{\mathbf{b}}$, the one of smallest
length — the **minimum-norm least-squares solution** — and the object that delivers it is
the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** $A^+$, with $\hat{\mathbf{c}}=A^+\mathbf{b}$ and
$\hat{\mathbf{b}}=AA^+\mathbf{b}$, where $AA^+$ is the genuine projection matrix even when
$A$ is rank-deficient. The pseudoinverse is constructed from the SVD and is the subject of
[GEOM-21]; for now the takeaway is that **projection always exists; the closed form
$P=A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ is the special case where the columns are independent.**

## "Orthogonal" is relative to the dot product you chose {#sec-oblique}

We have been measuring length and angle with the standard dot product
$\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}$. Everything — "perpendicular," "closest," symmetry of $P$ —
is relative to *that* choice. If the right notion of distance is a *weighted* one, say
$\mathbf{x}^\top W\mathbf{y}$ for some positive-definite weight matrix $W$ (which arises
whenever measurements have unequal reliability or are correlated), then the closest point
changes, and the projection that delivers it,
$P_W = A(A^\top W A)^{-1}A^\top W$, is idempotent but **no longer symmetric** in the
ordinary sense — it is an *oblique* projection from the standard viewpoint. This is the
geometry behind **generalized least squares** ([REGR-11]) and **whitening** ([GEOM-19]).
The lesson: projection is not absolute; it is defined by an inner product, and changing the
inner product changes the perpendicular.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If the columns of $A$ are already orthonormal, what is $P$?"* It collapses beautifully.
Orthonormal columns mean $A^\top A = I$ (each column has unit length, distinct columns are
perpendicular — [GEOM-05]), so $P=A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top = A\,I^{-1}A^\top = AA^\top$. No
inverse to compute, no conditioning to worry about, and the coefficients are just dot
products: $\hat c_j = \mathbf{a}_j^\top\mathbf{b}$. This is precisely why we *manufacture*
orthonormal bases — the **Gram–Schmidt** process and the **QR** factorization of [GEOM-07]
exist to replace the messy $A$ by an orthonormal $Q$ spanning the same space, turning
$P=A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ into the trivial, stable $P=QQ^\top$. The projection is
unchanged (same subspace, same closest point); only the arithmetic and its numerical
behavior improve.
:::

## What projection cannot tell you

Two honest non-failures worth stating, because interviewers probe them. First, projection
finds the closest point *in the subspace you handed it*; if the subspace is the wrong model
(a line where the truth curves), the projection is still computed flawlessly and is still
useless — exactly the nonlinearity failure of [REGR-01], now visible as "you projected onto
a column space that cannot represent the signal." Second, the residual length
$\lVert\mathbf{r}\rVert$ tells you *how much* of $\mathbf{b}$ the subspace fails to capture,
but not *why*; diagnosing that is the work of residual analysis ([REGR-03], [REGR-10]).
Projection is a precise answer to a narrow question — nearest point — and it is silent on
whether that was the right question.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[GEOM-05 *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*]**, which gave the
$S\oplus S^\perp$ decomposition and the Pythagorean theorem we leaned on in @thm-closest,
and from **[GEOM-04 *The Dot Product*]**, the source of every length and angle here. This
article is the **keystone**: it is the single operation the rest of the series reuses most.
From here the track continues:

- **[GEOM-07 *Gram–Schmidt and QR*]** — manufacture an orthonormal basis so projection
  becomes the trivial, stable $P=QQ^\top$. Gram–Schmidt is *this article's* line projection
  applied repeatedly, one direction at a time.
- **[GEOM-12 *The Four Fundamental Subspaces*]** — the residual orthogonal to the column
  space is the statement $\mathcal{C}(A)^\perp = \mathcal{N}(A^\top)$; this article is the
  first appearance of that pairing.
- **[GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21 *SVD and the Pseudoinverse*]** — the numerically sound, always-defined
  projection, which handles the rank-deficient case of @sec-rank-def and discharges the
  collinearity edge of @sec-ex-collinear.
- **[GEOM-25 *Gradient and Hessian of a Quadratic Form*]** — the calculus route to the
  normal equations: $\nabla\lVert\mathbf{b}-A\mathbf{c}\rVert^2 = 2A^\top(A\mathbf{c}-\mathbf{b})$,
  set to zero, *is* @eq-normal.

And it feeds directly into **Regression**: **[REGR-04 *Regression as Projection*]** and
**[REGR-05 *Multiple Regression*]** are this article with $\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{y}$ and $A=X$.
The normal equations @eq-normal are their $X^\top X\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}=X^\top\mathbf{y}$.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** Legendre's 1805 least-squares appendix and Gauss's 1809 *Theoria
  Motus* are the historical origin; the *geometric* reading — least squares as orthogonal
  projection — was crystallized in the twentieth century and is the organizing idea of
  Gilbert Strang's *Introduction to Linear Algebra* (the "projection is the heart of
  applied linear algebra" framing of this whole track).
- **The clean abstraction.** The closest-point theorem (@thm-closest) is the finite-dimensional
  case of the **Hilbert projection theorem**, which says the same thing for closed subspaces
  of any inner-product space — the foundation of Fourier series (projecting a function onto
  sines and cosines) and of conditional expectation in probability (projecting a random
  variable onto a $\sigma$-algebra). The geometry you learned here is *literally* the same;
  only the inner product changes.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *How do we project stably when columns are nearly
  dependent?* — QR and SVD, [GEOM-07] and [GEOM-20]. *What if the right distance is weighted?*
  — oblique projection and GLS, [REGR-11]. *What is the projection of a random variable?* —
  conditional expectation, owned by the Probability/Inference tracks, but it is exactly this
  picture with $\mathrm{Cov}$ as the inner product.
